speaker: charlie
text: Now here's a breakthrough that sounds like something out of Star Trek, but it's happening right here in Australia. <cite index='21-4,21-5'>University of Wollongong physicist Dr Enbang Li has demonstrated that gravity can subtly influence the behaviour of light, showing early experimental evidence that photons interact with Earth's gravitational field in measurable ways</cite>. Absolutely mind-blowing stuff.

speaker: alice
text: <cite index='21-6,21-7'>This could lead to more precise next-generation sensing technologies for environmental monitoring, navigation and underground mapping, with tiny shifts in gravity revealing critical changes like underground water levels or magma build-ups below volcanos</cite>. Imagine being able to predict volcanic eruptions just by measuring how light behaves differently.

speaker: charlie
text: The applications are incredible Jamie - from tracking glacier melt to locating mineral deposits to monitoring carbon storage underground. <cite index='21-9,21-10'>While it's still at proof-of-concept stage, the team's focusing on refining the measurement approach and exploring additional interactions between light and gravitational fields</cite>. Australian innovation at its finest right there.
